script:
Leo: Last time we walked through the eval plumbing, the harnesses and the hosting, all the pieces that have to line up before you can trust a single number. Today I want to go after a harder question. Say all of that plumbing works perfectly. You can run any eval you want, cheaply and reproducibly, and the number means the same thing every run. Which evals do you actually build. Because the team learned the hard way that the evals they already had were quietly missing the failures that mattered most, and no amount of good plumbing fixes an eval that is measuring the wrong thing.

Maya: Wait. If the evals already exist and they pass, why would they be missing anything? A passing test is a passing test. Is not the whole point that green means good?

Leo: That is the intuition I want to take apart, because it is exactly the one that burned them. The trap is hidden in the shape of the test, not in the plumbing. Almost every academic benchmark is single-turn. You hand the model one question, it produces one answer, and you grade that answer in isolation. There is no history, no memory, no back and forth. One prompt in, one response out, score it, move on. That shape is easy to run and easy to compare, which is exactly why the whole field standardized on it.

Maya: And what is the alternative. What does a multi-turn eval even look like?

Leo: A multi-turn agentic task carries state across several turns. The model is not answering a trivia question, it is doing a job that unfolds over a whole conversation. It might ask for a tool on one turn, get the result back on the next, and then have to use that result correctly two turns later. Everything that has been said, everything the tools returned, everything still pending, all of it has to survive across the turns. A single tool call can span turns. The request happens here, the answer comes back there, and using it correctly is a third moment entirely. The model has to remember and thread all of it.

Maya: Okay but here is what I do not get. If the model is good enough to score high on the single-turn test, why would it suddenly fall apart just because there are more turns? The knowledge is the same knowledge.

Leo: Because the two tests are measuring genuinely different things, and the single-turn score simply cannot see the multi-turn failure. Picture a model that scores wonderfully when you ask it one isolated question about how to use a tool. Now drop it into a real task where it has to call that tool, wait, read what came back, and act on it three turns later. It can drop the thread. It can forget what the tool returned. It can call the tool with all the right words and never actually thread the result into the next step. The single-turn eval never once asked it to hold state, so a model that cannot hold state still looks flawless. The score stays high while the agent quietly fails the real job.

Maya: So the single-turn number is not wrong exactly, it is just blind.

Leo: Blind is the perfect word for it. It is answering a much narrower question than the one you actually care about, and you mistake it for the whole picture. And this is not some hypothetical I am inventing. It is baked right into the research the team leans on. The Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard reached its third version precisely to close this gap. Earlier versions mostly checked whether the model emitted a well formed call in isolation. The third version added real multi-turn tasks and backend state checks, meaning it grades whether the actual state of the world ended up correct after a whole sequence of calls, not whether one call looked right on its own.

Maya: And the model the team is shipping, does it even claim to do multi-turn in the first place?

Leo: This is the detail that made the whole thing click for me. Look at the FunctionGemma documentation, the tool-calling guidance that ships with the model. It says the model supports single tool calls and parallel tool calls. It does not claim multi-turn, and it does not claim multi-step. So the documented, supported surface is exactly the single-turn slice, and the multi-turn behavior that real users lean on every day sits outside what anyone actually promised. The eval was written to match the promise instead of matching the use.

Maya: Hold on, that feels like the entire problem in one sentence. The test was written to match what they said the model does, not what people actually do with it. That cannot be an accident.

Leo: It is not an accident, and we will get to exactly why it happened, because the reason is more human than technical. But first let me tell you the story that proves how much this matters, because it is almost unbelievable. When Gemma 4 launched, its tool-call pass rate was around ten percent. Basically it looked, on paper, like a model that could barely call tools at all.

Maya: Ten percent. That is not a weak model, that is a broken one. Surely the only fix for a number that bad is to retrain it, right.

Leo: That is exactly what everyone assumed, and everyone was wrong. The weights were fine. The capability was already sitting in there the whole time. What was broken was the chat template, the formatting layer that wraps your prompt before it ever reaches the model. A hand-tuned template later lifted the pass rate dramatically, from that dismal ten percent up to something genuinely strong. Same weights. Nobody retrained a thing. They fixed the formatting, and the model that looked broken was suddenly good.

Maya: Okay, you have to slow down here. How can formatting possibly be worth that much? Explain what the template actually is and why it swings the number so hard.

Leo: Let me give you the clean decomposition, because this is the beating heart of the whole lesson. Think of a tool-calling model as two separate things stacked on top of each other. First there is capability, and capability lives in the weights. That is whether the model knows, in principle, which tool to reach for and with what arguments. Second there is protocol, and protocol lives in the chat template and the control tokens. That is the exact format, the special markers, the structure the model was taught to read and to emit so that a tool call can even be recognized and parsed by the surrounding system.

Maya: So capability is does it know the answer, and protocol is can it say that answer in the precise shape the system is expecting.

Leo: Precisely that. And here is the cruel part. If the protocol is even slightly off, the model can know the right tool, genuinely want to call it, and still fail every single grade, because the harness around it cannot parse what the model emitted, or the model never even sees the available tools in the format it was trained to recognize. A protocol bug hides a fully capable model behind a failing score. That ten percent was never a capability number. It was a protocol number wearing a capability number's clothes.

Maya: But then how did anyone ever figure that out? If the score just says ten percent, what on earth told them to go look at the template and not the weights.

Leo: This is my favorite part of the whole story. The academic single-turn evals could not tell the difference at all. They just reported a low number and shrugged, because a low number is all they can ever produce. What caught the nuance was Frank's own agentic benchmark, the multi-turn tool-calling suite. Because it exercised the model the way a real user actually would, across turns and real tool calls, it could show that the model's underlying choices were sound and that the failures were clustering around formatting rather than reasoning. That evidence is exactly what justified rolling out the template change. It was not a hunch or a vibe. The right eval turned a guess into proof, and that proof moved a real shipping decision.

Maya: Wait, back up a second. If multi-turn matters this much, how did the gap even exist in the first place. Somebody, somewhere, had to actively decide not to test it.

Leo: And that is the piece I find genuinely human. The gap existed because two teams disagreed about reality. The post-training team, the people who fine-tune the model, took the position that multi-turn was not really a supported use case, so why on earth would they be graded on it. The evals team, meanwhile, watched users lean on multi-turn constantly and wanted it measured. So the question was not even technical at first. It was a plain disagreement about what the model is fundamentally for.

Maya: So nobody was actually lying or being lazy. They just drew the boundary of the model in different places.

Leo: Exactly, and that is the lesson living underneath the lesson. What counts as a real eval is a decision, not a given. There is no law of nature that hands you the list of things you are supposed to measure. Somebody always chooses. And if the people doing the choosing decide that a whole category of real usage is out of scope, then the eval will look totally complete and still be stone blind in exactly that spot. The disagreement was not noise around the edges. It was the entire reason the ten percent story was able to happen at all.

Maya: That is kind of unsettling, honestly. The blind spot was not a mistake nobody noticed. It was a position somebody actually held on purpose.

Leo: Right, and once you truly see that, you start treating the choice of which evals to build as a first-class part of building the model, not an afterthought you bolt on at the end. Deciding what to measure is really deciding what the word good even means for this model.

Maya: Okay, so suppose you do decide to build a real multi-turn eval. How does it actually produce a score. What is the machinery underneath.

Leo: It builds directly on the seeded environment idea we talked through before. For an agentic task, the harness stands up a small fake world, seeded from a fixed and known starting state. Fake files, fake tools, a little fake database, all beginning in the exact same place every single run. Then it turns the model loose as an agent across as many turns as the task actually needs. And here is the crucial move, it grades at the very end by checking the state. Did the world end up the way it was supposed to. Not did the model say the right thing along the way, but did the model do the right thing, measured by the final state of that seeded environment. That end-state check is what makes a multi-turn score meaningful, because it survives everything messy that happened in between.

Maya: That makes sense to me. But now I am worried about something else entirely. If the eval is that specific, what stops the model from having simply memorized the answer. How do you know the score is real and not just the model recognizing a task it already saw during training.

Leo: You just put your finger on the thing that quietly destroys evals, contamination. If any of your eval data leaks into the training data, the model has effectively seen the exam before test day. Its score climbs, but that number is meaningless, because now it is measuring memorization instead of ability. And an inflated score is arguably far more dangerous than a low one, because it confidently tells you the model is ready to ship when it absolutely is not.

Maya: So how do you actually defend against that. You cannot exactly prove that a training set the size of the internet never once touched your test.

Leo: You practice held-out discipline. You deliberately keep the test data out of training, you hold it back behind a wall, and you guard the boundary between the two like it matters, because it does. You track where every eval set came from, you keep fresh held-out tasks the model could not possibly have memorized, and you treat any overlap between train and test as a contamination bug to be hunted down, not as a happy surprise when the score jumps. The entire value of an eval rests on the simple fact that the model has not seen it. Break that one fact, and the reproducible plumbing and the clever multi-turn design all combine to produce a beautiful, trustworthy looking lie.

Maya: So held-out is not bureaucracy or box-ticking. It is the thing that keeps the number honest in the first place.

Leo: It is the foundation the whole number stands on. A contaminated eval is not just a weaker eval, it is a broken one, and a broken eval that reads high is the worst possible outcome.

Maya: You said earlier that deciding what to measure is the real work. So let me ask directly. Is there an eval nobody has built yet that you think genuinely should exist.

Leo: There is one that Frank raised and that Sara thought was really worth exploring, and I love it precisely because it is not a capability test at all, it is a judgment test. Call it a context-disclosure privacy eval. The question it asks is simple to state and surprisingly hard to get right. How much of what an agent knows about you should it actually reveal, and to whom.

Maya: What do you mean by to whom. Information is just information. Either the agent knows a fact or it does not.

Leo: That is exactly the assumption this eval attacks. Think about your own life for a second. There are things you would happily tell your wife that you would never say to a colleague. There are things a close friend can know that a total stranger absolutely should not. The right amount of disclosure is not a fixed quantity, it depends entirely on who is asking. A good agent, one that acts on your behalf, has to respect that boundary. It should reveal one level of context to a spouse, a different level to a friend, another again to a coworker, and almost nothing to a stranger.

Maya: And an eval could actually score that. How would you even begin to grade something that squishy and human.

Leo: You would seed scenarios, the same way the agentic tasks work. Take the same underlying private context about a user, then have four different people ask about it, a wife, a friend, a colleague, and a stranger. The eval checks whether the agent modulates what it shares to match who is on the other side. Reveal too much to the stranger and it fails on privacy. Clam up completely with the spouse when openness was clearly appropriate and it fails on usefulness. The score measures whether the agent respects the boundary, disclosing appropriately for each audience instead of treating every asker the same.

Maya: That is a completely different kind of test from tool calling. It is almost about manners.

Leo: It is, and that is exactly the point. It is worth building, and it is the sort of thing the team will explore more later on. But notice what it shares with everything else we talked about today. It only means anything if it exercises the model the way a real user would actually be exposed, with genuinely different audiences and real private stakes on the line. Same thread runs through all of it.

Maya: So if I pull the whole thing together. The evals that matter are not the ones that are easy to write. They are the ones that put the model into the exact situation the user actually puts it in.

Leo: That is the entire lesson in one sentence. Single-turn looks complete and misses the multi-turn failures. A ten percent score looked like a broken model and was really just a formatting bug, and only the agentic eval could tell the difference. The blind spots came from a decision about what to measure, contamination can quietly inflate any score you trust, and the most interesting evals still left to build, like the privacy one, are judgment tests nobody has written yet. Designing those tests is not busywork around the model. Designing the evals that exercise the model the way people really use it is itself real model-building work.

Maya: The thing I will actually keep is the ten percent again, but for a brand new reason this time. Same weights, a template fix, and a broken looking model turns out to be completely fine. The only reason anyone ever knew was that somebody had built the right eval to see it.

Leo: Hold onto that one. The model was never the problem. The missing eval was. Build the tests that match how people really use the thing, keep them honest and held out, and you stop shipping blind. That, in the end, is the job.

scene.title: Agentic evals that matter: multi-turn, the template trap, and what to measure
scene.description: Orientation for eval design rather than eval plumbing: why single-turn scores stay high while multi-turn agents fail, how a ten percent tool-call rate was a chat-template protocol bug and not a capability gap, why two teams disagreed over whether multi-turn was even in scope, how a seeded environment grades on final state, why contamination and held-out discipline keep a score honest, and a preview of a context-disclosure privacy eval worth building.

panels.title: Single-turn versus multi-turn evaluation
panels.description: The two eval shapes and why one is blind to the failure the other exposes, grounded in the benchmarks the team leans on.

data.label: single-turn
data.value: one question in, one answer graded in isolation, no state

data.label: multi-turn agentic
data.value: state carried across turns, a tool call can span turns

data.label: the blind spot
data.value: single-turn score stays high while the multi-turn agent fails

data.label: BFCL version three
data.value: adds multi-turn tasks and backend state checks

data.label: FunctionGemma docs
data.value: single and parallel calls supported, multi-turn and multi-step not

panels.title: The template trap: capability versus protocol
panels.description: The decomposition that explains how a capable model scored ten percent, and how the right eval caught the nuance the academic ones missed.

data.label: capability in the weights
data.value: the model already knows which tool to call

data.label: protocol in the template
data.value: chat template and control tokens carry the format

data.label: protocol bug
data.value: ten percent pass rate, capable model hidden behind it

data.label: hand-tuned template
data.value: same weights, pass rate lifted dramatically

data.label: agentic benchmark
data.value: proved formatting not capability, justified the rollout

panels.title: What makes a score honest and worth building
panels.description: How a seeded environment grades on final state, why held-out discipline defeats contamination, and the privacy eval preview.

data.label: seeded environment
data.value: fixed start, agent acts, grade the final state

data.label: what to measure is a decision
data.value: two teams disagreed on whether multi-turn was in scope

data.label: contamination
data.value: eval data in training inflates the score to a lie

data.label: held-out discipline
data.value: keep test data out of training, guard the boundary

data.label: context-disclosure privacy eval
data.value: reveal by audience, wife versus stranger, worth building

cues.label: Which evals to build
cues.headline: Good plumbing cannot save a wrong test
cues.narration: Even with perfect reproducible plumbing, the team found their existing evals were quietly missing the failures that mattered most.
cues.transform: ask which evals are worth building
cues.pass: the gap between passing and actually good

cues.label: Single-turn versus multi-turn
cues.headline: One shape is blind to the other
cues.narration: Single-turn grades one answer in isolation, while a multi-turn agentic task carries state across turns and a tool call can span turns.
cues.transform: put the model in a multi-turn task
cues.pass: a failure the single-turn test never saw

cues.label: The benchmarks that expose it
cues.headline: Grade the world, match the use
cues.narration: The Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard version three adds multi-turn and state checks, while FunctionGemma docs promise only single and parallel calls.
cues.transform: check backend state, not one call
cues.pass: an eval matched to real usage

cues.label: The template trap
cues.headline: Ten percent was a formatting bug
cues.narration: At the Gemma 4 launch tool calls passed around ten percent, but the weights were fine and a hand-tuned chat template lifted the rate dramatically.
cues.pass: a capable model hidden behind a template

cues.label: What caught the nuance
cues.headline: The right eval turned guess into proof
cues.narration: Academic single-turn evals only reported a low number, while Frank's agentic benchmark showed the failures were formatting and justified the rollout.
cues.transform: exercise the model like a user
cues.pass: evidence that moved a shipping decision

cues.label: Why the teams disagreed
cues.headline: What counts as a real eval is a decision
cues.narration: The post-training team said multi-turn was out of scope while the evals team saw users depend on it, so the blind spot was a position somebody held.
cues.receive: a disagreement about the model's purpose
cues.transform: treat the choice of evals as first-class
cues.pass: the reason the gap could exist at all

cues.label: Keeping the score honest
cues.headline: Seeded state and held-out discipline
cues.narration: A seeded environment grades on final state, and holding eval data out of training defeats contamination that would inflate the number into a lie.
cues.transform: grade final state, guard the boundary
cues.pass: a score you can actually trust

cues.label: An eval worth building
cues.headline: Disclosure by audience
cues.narration: A context-disclosure privacy eval would score whether an agent reveals the right amount to a wife, a friend, a colleague, or a stranger, worth exploring later.
cues.pass: a preview of a judgment eval to build
